title: Souvlaki — lamb
subtitle: Lamb skewers marinated in garlic, cumin, and lemon
excerpt: Lamb shoulder cut into cubes and marinated in lemon juice, garlic, cumin, dried oregano, and olive oil, then grilled on skewers over high heat until charred at the edges and pink at the centre. The classic version for Easter and special occasions.
sourceNotes: Lamb souvlaki is the original Greek version of the dish — lamb has been the primary meat of Greek cooking since antiquity, and lamb skewers grilled over wood or charcoal are documented from the Homeric period. The pork version became dominant in central and northern Greece in the postwar period as pork farming expanded; lamb souvlaki remains the Easter standard throughout the country.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate for up to 24 hours in the fridge.

content.text: Lamb shoulder has enough fat and flavour to hold up to the high, direct heat of a grill where leaner lamb leg would dry out. The marinade includes cumin alongside the oregano — a small addition that deepens the lamb's own flavour without tipping into Middle Eastern territory. The lamb should be served pink: an internal temperature of 62 to 65°C after resting.

Aim for uniform cubes so all pieces cook evenly. If the shoulder has a thick fat cap, leave most of it on: it protects the meat during grilling and renders off partially, basting the skewer as it cooks.

Combine the {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{garlic}}, {{oregano-dried}}, {{cumin-ground}}, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Add the {{lamb-shoulder}} cubes and toss to coat. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight.

Thread the lamb onto skewers. Heat a barbecue, grill, or griddle pan to its highest temperature. Grill for 10 to 12 minutes, turning every 3 minutes, until well charred on the outside and pink at the centre. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve in warm {{pitta-bread}} with {{red-onion}} and {{lemon}} wedges.

items.symptom: Lamb is dry and grey throughout
items.cause: Leg used instead of shoulder, or overcooked past 70°C
items.fix: Use shoulder for skewers; stop at 62–65°C internal; the lamb continues cooking as it rests

items.symptom: Fat has not rendered and is chewy
items.cause: Not enough direct heat, or pieces too large
items.fix: High, direct heat is needed to render the fat quickly; cut pieces no larger than 4 cm

items.symptom: Marinade has made the surface mushy
items.fix: 24 hours is the maximum; more denatures the surface proteins

items.cause: Mutton used, or very mature lamb
items.fix: For skewers, use young lamb shoulder; the marinade mellows but does not eliminate the flavour of older animals

content.text: Lamb souvlaki is the Easter version — Greece's most important food occasion centres on whole roast lamb on the spit (kokoretsi and the whole animal over coals), and smaller lamb skewers are part of the same celebration. Outside the Easter period, lamb souvlaki appears alongside pork in traditional souvladzidika and at tavernas. The Aegean islands, including Crete and the Dodecanese, maintain stronger lamb-and-goat traditions than the mainland, and lamb souvlaki is more common there year-round.
